data.title: United Airlines: Premium Ambitions Through Economic Turbulence
data.description:
Introduction

Over the last year or so, United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL) has been enjoying some major tailwinds, with record earnings, a surge in international traffic, and a successful pivot towards a more premium product offering. However, with economic turbulence building, questions remain on whether this rally can continue, or if some cooling off is overdue.

Despite the risks, I'm cautiously bullish looking ahead. With United holding on to some major sectoral advantages, and looking strong in execution plans, I think there could still be a decent amount of value in UAL shares.

Company Overview

United Airlines is obviously a household name in the aviation space, as one of the original commercial airlines in the U.S. market. In 2024, the firm carried more than 150 million passengers.

With operations running out of Chicago, and over 109,200 employees, the company's extensive network is supported by hubs in Newark, Houston, San Francisco, Denver and Washington D.C. It participates in the Star Alliance, enabling a range of joint ventures, and international codeshare routes across other airlines. As of 2024, the firm sat in the top three of the domestic market by market share.

Over the last few years, management has focussed on upgrading the customer experience to a more premium level, both at the gates, on the flight, in the lounge, and on the booking page.

United Next: First-Class Ambition

The company's "United Next" campaign looks to take significant steps. With over 660 new aircraft on delivery, management is determined to boost seat capacity, but with a clear focus on the high-spending passenger.

The United Polaris experience aims to bring a new standard of quality to premium cabin travel on Boeing 787-9 aircraft from 2026. These suites will include sliding doors, impressive 4K display screens, luxury bedding, and premium food selections in specific markets. In a competitive environment where many domestic and international providers are offering such products and services in increasing numbers, management seem to understand this is a necessity.

An expansion in the technology available on board is also seemingly a priority, with United offering free, fast Wi-Fi following a partnership with SpaceX's Starlink.

The Numbers Behind the Strategy

The airline posted a solid $13.2 billion in Q1 2025 revenues, breaking a Q1 record. EPS also came in above analyst estimates $0.77 at $0.91, indicating the strong demand for premium cabins, and in strong operating leverage.

I'm also encouraged to see operating cash flow climbing by 30% from the previous year to $3.7 billion. Like many aviation firms, it carries fairly substantial debts of $32.9 billion. Considering cash and short-term investments, United a net debt position of roughly $17.6 billion. This puts the airline's net leverage ratio at about 2.3x trailing EBITDA, which, while elevated, feels manageable given current cash generation.

Looking closer at how the company's premium strategy is progressing, I'm pleased to see the 9.2% revenue growth in premium cabins outstrip growth in overall capacity. To me, this suggests that the campaign is working to convince passengers to pay more for a better experience. With links being built in the credit card space, such as the MileagePlus program with JPMorgan Chase, this could be a major area to drive further growth over the coming years.

Upside Potential for UAL

United trades at just 7.6x forward earnings and an EV/EBITDA ratio of 3.5x. Compared to Delta (at roughly 9.8x forward earnings) and international rivals like Lufthansa (6.9x) or IAG (7.1x), United remains attractively priced, even after its recent rally.

From a discounted cash flow (DCF) perspective, United's valuation looks even more compelling:

  • Annualised revenue based on Q1 2025 = ~$52.8 billion
  • Free cash flow margin = 6.5%, yielding ~$3.25 billion FCF
  • WACC (discount rate) = 8.0%
  • Terminal growth rate = 2.0% (in line with global GDP growth rates, and inflation targets by most central banks)
  • Shares outstanding = ~329 million

This produces a total equity value of $45 billion, with an intrinsic value of approximately $128 per share, suggesting about 60% upside from the current level. To illustrate valuation sensitivity, the following analysis was conducted:

Scenario WACC FCF Margin Growth Rate Intrinsic Value/Share
Bearish 9.0% 5.5% 2.5% $84
Base 8.0% 6.5% 4.0% $128
Bullish 7.5% 8.0% 5.0% $156

Based on this analysis, even the conservative case offers some margin of safety. This excludes the incremental boost from margin expansion, improved credit card economics, and capacity leverage through United's premium-heavy international routes. It also largely excludes the seasonality of the sector, which is worth being conscious of as an investor.

Wall Street generally holds a similarly bullish approach to the shares, with a Buy rating accompanied by an average price target of $98.

Buybacks add another lever. United repurchased $451 million in stock during Q1, reducing the float, while earnings remain stable. Assuming this pace continues, EPS could grow organically by 3-4% annually through share count reduction alone, creating steady intrinsic value growth even in neutral macro conditions.

The Road Ahead

There's no such thing as certainty in the airline sector, with any number of variables likely to impact the sector over the coming years. Part of United's approach to shift towards premium is about building a more resilient revenue base. Clearly, business and luxury travel passengers are willing to pay a premium for a better quality experience, and by moving into these higher-margin areas of the market, management has more room to manoeuvre.

Building out international markets also offers some level of diversification against domestic slowdowns. This potentially puts the company apart from other domestic providers, who are highly exposed to consumer dynamics in the U.S.

However, it's important to focus on the plethora of challenges on the horizon for many companies in the sector. My primary area of worry is the changing sentiment from analysts, with 15 EPS downgrades against zero upgrades, suggesting that enthusiasm may be running out after the recent rally.

This is easy to understand, with inflation remaining a constant issue, and with labour issues a persistent threat to investor confidence, there is an ever-present chance of strikes or large-scale dispute.

I think it's also worth keeping an eye on the debt level over the coming months. While there is plenty of cash on the balance sheet to cover this, such enormous growth in fleet expansion and the premium elements of the strategy are going to require plenty of cash over the coming years. The ongoing rollout of these plans will require close work with suppliers, regulators, and internal teams. If deliveries, or plans look to be delayed, the investment case could weaken significantly.

This, combined with volatile fuel costs, could be a challenge as geopolitical tensions continue to escalate. United chooses not to hedge fuel, meaning that an approach is taken to manage volatility through fare adjustments and other controls, but this is far from the perfect solution.

Moving in the Right Direction

United's management is clearly not content being just one of many in the domestic and international space. With a strong pipeline, and a strategy built on high-margin customers, it could be on the right path to elevating both reputation and revenues over the coming years.

With a valuation which I see having a good level of upside, based on market volatility rather than execution, I see investors willing to look past near-term challenges and negativity seeing success. While there are risks, and companies in the airline sector are rarely for the faint-hearted, I see enough potential to take a cautious Buy rating on the company.

data.title: Central Pacific Financial: Margin Likely To Push Up Earnings, But Valuation Is Not Attractive
data.description:
Central Pacific Financial Corp.'s (NYSE:CPF) stock price has dropped by ~8.5% since I adopted a hold rating in my last report. As I will discuss further, my analysis shows that the price drop, which was driven by the trade war, was justified. Due to the tariffs, I'm increasing my provision expense, thereby reducing my earnings estimate for this year by 4% to $2.30 per share. Although I'm revising downward my earnings estimate, I still believe that the earnings for this year will be higher than the earnings for last year. The net interest margin's continued expansion will be the biggest earnings catalyst. Based on a negative total expected return, which includes a good dividend yield of 4.0%, I'm maintaining a hold rating on Central Pacific Financial.

Turnaround for Loans Still Not in Sight

After declining for seven consecutive quarters, the loan portfolio finally stabilized in the first quarter of 2025. However, I don't think this is the start of a turnaround. I think the loan growth will continue to remain neutral to slightly negative because of the current operating environment.

Central Pacific Financial operates mostly in Hawaii. By loan type, the loan book is somewhat diverse. However, it does have a significant exposure to residential properties as taken together, residential mortgages and home equity lines of credit ("HELOC") make up ~47% of total loans.

Home prices in Hawaii have flattened recently, which is good for the residential segment.

On the other hand, mortgage rates have risen as Treasury yields have heightened amid the trade war. This bodes ill for growth of the residential mortgage segment.

Central Pacific Financial does not have much direct exposure to tourism, with hotels making up just 11% of the commercial real estate segment, and 3% of total loans. Nevertheless, tourism is a big contributor to Hawaii's GDP, with the state's government estimating its total impact as almost a quarter of the total GDP.

Therefore, the state of the tourism industry is a key factor affecting the Hawaiian economy, and consequently the demand for CPF's credit products. Despite the trade war, the flow of international visitors has not slowed down. The number of visitors is roughly the same as last year. However, it's still far from the pre-pandemic level.

Considering these factors, I'm expecting the net loan balance to remain almost unchanged through the end of this year.

Rate Cuts to Depress the Deposit Cost and Raise the Margin

The net interest margin grew by an impressive 14 basis points in the first quarter of the year, which beat my expectations. The margin has now grown in the last four consecutive quarters. The management is expecting the margin to expand by a further 4–7 basis points in the second quarter. The management mentioned in the conference call that it expects additional Fed funds rate cuts to benefit the net interest margin, as the deposits still have some downward re-pricing ability.

Non-maturing interest-bearing deposits made up 56% of total deposits at the end of March, as shown in the chart below, taken from the earnings presentation. These deposits will re-price downward soon after rate cuts. How much they re-price depends on the competition for deposits in the market.

Despite the inflationary nature of the tariffs, I think the Fed will still go for a 50-basis points rate cut in the second half of this year. I believe the tariffs will hurt economic growth, that is why the Fed will need to cut rates.

Due to the anticipated rate cuts and the nature of the deposit portfolio, I'm expecting the net interest margin to grow by 10 basis points in the last three quarters of 2025.

Hiking the Provisions Expense Estimate Despite the Management's Guidance

The management mentioned in the conference call that around 10% of the total loan book is affected by the trade war. However, the management seemed to downplay the impact of the trade policy uncertainty on its loan portfolio's performance, saying that the government's policy actions "are not intended to damage the economy."

I believe that even if an action is not intended to harm something, the action can still unintentionally harm it. Therefore, I'm not going to be as carefree as the management. I'm going to assume a jump in the provision expense this year based on CPF's history. I think the level seen in 2020 is too extreme; therefore, I'm going to take a provision level that is somewhere in the middle.

Overall, I'm estimating a provision expense of $25 million this year, or 0.48% of loans. In my last report, I projected a provision expense of $12 million for 2025.

One factor that gives me hope is that 75% of the total loan balance is secured by real estate. So I think it's highly unlikely that the company's net income would turn negative because of the provisions expense for loan losses.

Reducing the Earnings Growth Optimism

Previously, I was expecting a significant jump in earnings this year. However, due to the trade war, I will now have to temper my estimate. Nevertheless, the earnings for 2025 will likely still be higher than last year's earnings due to continued net interest margin expansion.

On the other hand, the jump in provision expense for loan losses will likely drag earnings. Meanwhile, I'm not expecting any material loan growth this year, so this will not be a factor. Further, I'm assuming the non-interest income to be the same as last year, with the same kind of mark-to-market losses on held-to-maturity securities (I will discuss the loss on available-for-sale securities in the next section).

Meanwhile, the non-interest expense will likely be lower this year because of the strict expense discipline seen over the last two quarters. The expense run rate actually declined from $47 million in 3Q-2024 to $42 million in 1Q-2025. The management is planning to consolidate its office space to save an estimated $1 million in operating costs annually, as mentioned in the conference call. However, the consolidation will result in a one-time write-off of around $2.0 million to $2.5 million in the second or third quarter of this year. Taking these measures into account, I'm anticipating a provision expense of around $170 million in 2025, down by 1.8% from last year's level.

Overall, I'm expecting the company to report an EPS of $2.30 per share for 2025, up by 17% year-over-year. My updated EPS estimate is below my previous estimate of $2.40 largely because I've revised upward the provision expense estimate.

Bond Market Turbulence to Reduce Equity

The Treasury bond yields have risen over the past week because of Moody's U.S. credit rating downgrade as well as a disappointing 20-year treasury auction, as per news reports. Moreover, the sentiments of bond investors have worsened because of the tax and spending bill, which is expected to add $3.3 trillion in debt through 2034.

Central Pacific Financial has a sizable securities portfolio, with $780 million worth of Available-for-Sale ("AFS") and $590 million worth of Held-to-Maturity securities. If bond yields continue to rise, then the market value of CPF's securities will fall. In the case of AFS securities, the unrealized mark-to-market loss will bypass the income statement and flow directly into the equity account, thereby reducing it.

During 2022, when the upper limit of the Fed funds rate rose from 0.25% to 4.50%, Central Pacific Financial recorded a rise in unrealized losses of around $100 million. Taking guidance from history, I'm expecting $10 million of unrealized losses to erode the equity book value in the second quarter of 2025. The following table shows my estimates for loans, securities, equity, and other key balance sheet line items.

Maintaining a Hold Rating

In my last report, which I wrote in October 2024, I adopted a hold rating on Central Pacific Financial with a December 2025 target price of $26.3. Since then, the stock has declined by ~8.5% mostly due to the tariffs.

Central Pacific Financial is offering a good dividend yield of 4.0% at a quarterly dividend per share of $0.27. To determine the potential price upside, we need to consider the historical price-to-tangible book ("P/TB") and price-to-earnings ("P/E") multiples, given below.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with the forecast earnings per share of $2.30 gives a target price of $24.3 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with the forecast tangible book value per share of $20.6 gives a target price of $25.1 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $24.7, which is 7.6% below the closing market price as of May 21, 2025. Adding the dividend yield to this price downside gives a total expected return of negative 3.7%. As a result, I'm going to maintain a hold rating on Central Pacific Financial.

data.title: Tracking Bill Ackman's Pershing Square 13F Portfolio - Q1 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Pershing Square's 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Ackman's regulatory 13F Form filed on 05/15/2025. Please visit our Tracking Bill Ackman's Pershing Square Holdings article for an idea on how his holdings have progressed over the years and our previous update for the fund's moves during Q4 2024.

Ackman's 13F portfolio value decreased from ~$12.61B this quarter to ~$11.93B. The number of positions increased from 9 to 10. The portfolio remains heavily concentrated, with a few huge bets. The top three positions account for ~50% of the total portfolio value: Uber Technologies, Brookfield Corp, and Alphabet.

In addition to partner stakes, the fund also invests the capital from Pershing Square Holdings (OTCPK:PSHZF), a public entity that debut in Euronext Amsterdam in October 2014. This was set up primarily to increase the amount of capital invested that is permanent. Pershing Square Holdings had underperformed the S&P 500 since its EOY 2012 inception. This changed in 2019 as they compounded at ~50% in the three years through 2021. Their original flagship fund's (2004 inception) track record is excellent, with annualized returns of ~15.9% compared to ~10% for the S&P 500 index. YTD as of mid-May, they are up 7.6%.

Note 1: They established a new stake in Amazon (AMZN) after the quarter ended.

Note 2: Pershing Square Holdings has always traded at a discount to NAV. It currently trades at ~$52 per share, compared to a NAV of ~$77.

Note 3: Pershing Square has a ~6.2% stake in Universal Music Group (OTCPK:UMGNF) at a cost-basis of ~€18 per share. It is down from ~10% as of last quarter, It currently trades at ~€28. It is still their largest equity stake. The position is not in their 13F report, as it is not US listed. Pershing Square said the following regarding the reduction in UMG:

UMG has substantially outperformed the rest of our portfolio (other than the GSEs) year to date in light of its extremely strong recent results. UMG was our largest position going into the day at more than ~27% of our capital. After the sale, it will still be our largest position at about ~17% of the portfolio."

Note 4: Pershing Square Tontine Holdings (PSTH), the liquidated SPAC, distributed SPARC shares (not tradable currently) to previous PSTH common and warrant holders following its successful registration during Q3 2023.

Note 5: Pershing Square filed to offer a new US listed closed-end fund during early 2024. The offer was withdrawn at the end of July following lack of interest.

To learn more about Bill Ackman, check-out the book "Confidence Game: How Hedge Fund Manager Bill Ackman Called Wall Street's Bluff".

New Stakes:

Uber Technologies (UBER): UBER is currently the top position in the 13F portfolio at 18.5%. It was purchased this quarter at prices between ~$61 and ~$82. The stock currently trades at ~$88.

Note: Their cost-basis is in the high-60s. The investment thesis is based on the idea that ride hailing won't be a winner-takes-all business. In that context, Uber will have a central role to play.

Hertz Global (HTZ): The 0.50% HTZ stake was established this quarter at prices between $3.26 and ~$4.74 and the stock currently trades at $7.26.

Note: Their cost-basis as ~$3.80 and ownership stake as ~20%. They also highlighted its asymmetric range of outcomes and several fold return potential during their Q1 2025 investor call today.

Stake Disposals:

Nike, Inc. (NKE): The large 11.26% NKE stake was purchased during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$75 and ~$97. Most of the position was built during the next quarter at prices between $70.77 and $89.44. The last quarter saw a ~15% stake increase at prices between $72.62 and $88.23. The disposal this quarter was at prices between ~$63 and ~$82. It is now at $61.32.

Note: their Q4 2024 letter mentioned the long exposure to Nike was kept the same by buying leaps:

In early 2025, we converted our NIKE common equity position to deep in-the-money call options. This structure allows us to maintain exposure to the upside potential of owning the stock outright, while unlocking capital for new investments. The positive intrinsic value of these options and their low break-even price minimizes the likelihood of loss, while the multi-year term provides duration through NIKE's turnaround, which, we believe, will ultimately be successful, but may lead to short-term share price volatility. In a successful turnaround, the option payoffs should be more than double the returns from owning the common stock. We continue to believe that NIKE has the potential to be one of the great large-cap consumer turnarounds."

Stake Increases:

Brookfield Corp. (BN): BN is now a large (top three) position at ~18% of the portfolio. The stake was established during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$38 and ~$45. The bulk of the position was built during the next quarter at prices between $40.86 and $53.42. There was a ~18% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$49 and ~$63. The stock currently trades at $57.17.

Stake Decreases:

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG): GOOG is now the third largest 13F stake at ~14% of the portfolio. It was purchased last March in the low-90s price range. There was a ~13% stake increase during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$104 and ~$128. That was followed by a ~20% increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$117 and ~$139. Q2 2024 saw a ~16% trimming at prices between ~$152 and ~$187. The stock currently trades at ~$172. There was a ~7% trimming this quarter.

Note: They increased their position after the quarter ended at ~20% lower prices compared to their reductions during Q2 2024 and this quarter.

Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG): CMG is now a ~9% of the portfolio position. The stake was established in Q3 2016 at a cost-basis of ~$8.10 per share. The position was sold down by ~30% in Q3 2018 in the high-single-digit price-range and that was followed with a ~17% trimming over the next four quarters. Q1 2020 also saw a one-third selling at ~$17.20 per share. There was a ~7% trimming in Q1 2021 at ~$26.80 per share. Q3 2021 saw a ~3% increase at ~$38.20 per share. There was a ~14% trimming over the two quarters through Q2 2023 at prices between ~$27.30 and ~$42.80. Q4 2023 saw a ~14% trimming at prices between ~$35.60 and ~$46.80. The next quarter also saw a ~10% trimming at prices between ~$44.30 and ~$59.10. Q2 2024 saw another ~22% reduction at prices between ~$57 and ~$69. That was followed by a ~14% selling during the last quarter at prices between $55.73 and $66.16. This quarter saw a ~13% further reduction at prices between $47.55 and $61.16. The stock currently trades at $50.78.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the 50-for-1 stock split in June 2024.

Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP): CP is an 8.71% of the portfolio position. A small stake was purchased during Q4 2021 at prices between ~$64.50 and ~$78. Q3 2022 saw the stake built to a large position at prices between ~$67 and ~$83. The stock currently trades at $80.40. There was marginal trimming this quarter.

Note: The entire position was sold after the quarter ended, according to their Q1 2025 investor call today.

Hilton Worldwide Holdings (HLT): The 5.72% portfolio stake was established in October 2018. It was purchased at prices between $64 and $78. Q1 2020 saw a ~30% stake increase at a cost-basis of ~$70. Q1 2022 saw a ~20% selling at prices between ~$129 and ~$158. There was a ~7% trimming during Q1 2023 while Q3 2023 saw a ~10% increase. The next quarter saw a ~11% selling at prices between ~$147 and ~$182. There was a ~18% trimming during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$202 and ~$234. That was followed by a ~26% reduction during the last quarter at prices between $229.56 and $258.32. There was a ~45% selling this quarter at prices between ~$219 and ~$275. The stock currently trades at ~$247.

Note 1: Their overall cost-basis is ~$72 per share.

Note 2: In Q4 2018, Hilton Worldwide Holdings came back into the portfolio after a gap of eighteen months. The previous position was purchased in Q3 2016 and disposed a year later. Pershing Square has said that the new position was acquired at a better valuation compared to their previous purchase. Also, the business structure has transformed into a capital-light model following the spinoff in early 2017 of Park Hotels & Resorts (PK) and Hilton Grand Vacations (HGV).

Kept Steady:

Restaurant Brands International (QSR): The QSR stake is a large ~13% of the portfolio stake. Pershing Square's original cost-basis was ~$16. Q3 2017 saw a ~32% selling at prices between $59 and $66. That was followed with a ~22% reduction in H1 2018 at prices between $53 and $64. The four quarters through Q3 2019 also saw a ~28% selling at prices between $52 and $79. In June 2020, they increased the position by roughly two-thirds through forward contracts at $44.20 cost-basis. The stock currently trades at $69.43.

Note: They own ~7.4% of the business.

Howard Hughes Holdings (HHH): HHH is now a ~12% of the 13F portfolio position. The stake was first established in 2010 as a result of its spin-off from GGP Inc. The majority of the current stake is from the addition of ~10M shares in Q1 2020 at ~$50 per share through a private placement. Q1 2021 saw a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$78 and ~$102. Q4 2022 saw another ~17% stake increase at an average cost of ~$72 per share. Q4 2023 saw a ~12% stake increase at prices between ~$66 and ~$87. The stock is currently at $67.06.

Note: Earlier this month, they disclosed acquiring 9M shares of HHH at $100 per share and plans to transform the company into a diversified holding company with an insurance operation in the style of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A).

Seaport Entertainment (SEG): SEG was spun-off from Howard Hughes in early August 2024. Shareholders received one share for every nine shares of HHH held. It started trading at ~$22 and currently goes for $17.63. There was a ~20% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between $26.12 and $33.86.

Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) and Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) are other long positions in the partnership—the holdings were disclosed in 13D filings on November 15, 2013—as they are not 13F securities, they are not listed in the 13F report. Ackman held just under 10% of the outstanding shares of both these businesses—115.57M shares of FNMA at a cost-basis of $2.29 and 63.5M shares of FMCC at a cost-basis of $2.14. The combined investment outlay was ~$400M. FNMA currently trades at $11.22 & FMCC at $8.05 per share. In March 2018, Pershing Square said their Fannie/Freddie pfds now amounts to 21% of the total investment in the two GSEs. Their Q1 2025 investor call today mentioned the following:

Fannie/Freddie common shares could become an anchor for the sovereign wealth fund as they are uniquely situated as forever assets that can grow, given growth is the only way for the country's deficit problem - was expecting common shares to go up more today. The low-30s price estimate provided in our January presentation upon successful exit from conservatorship may turn out to be low, given Pulte's operational, management, and board changes which are solid steps toward running them as real private businesses rather than government mandated.

The spreadsheet below highlights changes to Pershing Square's 13F stock holdings in Q1 2025:

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Precious Metals Royalty And Streaming Companies: The April Report
data.description:
Precious metals royalty and streaming companies represent a very interesting sub-industry of the precious metals mining industry. They provide some leverage to the growing metals prices, similar to the typical mining companies; however, they are less risky in comparison to them. Their incomes are derived from royalty and streaming agreements. Under a metal streaming agreement, the streaming company provides an upfront payment to acquire the right to future deliveries of a predefined percentage of the metal production of a mining operation.

The streaming company also pays some ongoing payments that are usually well below the market price of the metal. They can be set as a fixed sum (e.g., $300/toz gold) or as a percentage (e.g., 20% of the prevailing gold price), or a combination of both (e.g., the lower of a) $300/toz gold and b) 20% of the prevailing gold price). The royalties usually apply to a small fraction of the mining project production (usually 1-3%), and they are not connected with ongoing payments. They can have various forms, but the most common is a small percentage of the net smelter return ("NSR"). The NSR is calculated as revenues from the sale of the mined products minus transportation and refining costs.

To better track the overall performance of the whole sub-industry, I created a capitalization-weighted index (the Precious Metals Royalty and Streaming Index) consisting of 11 companies (in June 2020, expanded to 15). Later, based on the inquiries of readers, I also introduced an equal-weighted version of the index. Until March 2021, both indices included the same companies and were calculated back to January 2019.

However, some major changes occurred in April 2021. Due to the boom of the royalty and streaming industry and the emergence of many new companies, the indices experienced two major changes. First of all, the market capitalization-weighted index was modified to include only the 5 biggest companies: Franco-Nevada (FNV), Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), Royal Gold (RGLD), Osisko Gold Royalties (OR), and Sandstorm Gold (SAND). The combined weight of these 5 companies on the old index was around 95%, therefore, the small companies had only a negligible impact on their performance. The values of the index were re-calculated back to January 2019, and between January 2019 and March 2021, the difference in the overall performance of the old and the new index was only 2.29 percentage points. The second change is related to the equally weighted index that was expanded to 20 companies.

The previous editions of the monthly report can be found here: May 2019, June 2019, July 2019, August 2019, September 2019, October 2019, November 2019, December 2019, January 2020, February 2020, March 2020, April 2020, May 2020, June 2020, July 2020, August 2020, September 2020, October 2020, November 2020, December 2020, January 2021, February 2021, March 2021, April 2021, May 2021, June 2021, July 2021, August 2021, September 2021, October 2021, November 2021, December 2021, January 2022, February 2022, March 2022, April 2022, May 2022, June 2022, July 2022, August 2022, September 2022, October 2022, November 2022, December 2022, January 2023, February 2023, March 2023, April 2023, May 2023, June 2023, July 2023, August 2023, September 2023, October 2023, November 2023, December 2023, January 2024, February 2024, March 2024, April 2024, May 2024, June 2024, July 2024, August 2024, September 2024, October 2024, November 2024, December 2024, January 2025, February 2025, March 2025.

As of the end of April, Wheaton Precious Metals remained the biggest of the precious metals R&S companies, with a market capitalization of $37.9 billion. Franco-Nevada and Royal Gold followed with $33.1 billion and $12 billion respectively. At the bottom of the ranking remained Silver Crown Royalties (SCRI:CA) valued only at $11.6 million. Several changes occurred in the ranking during April. First of all, Orogen (OTCQX:OGNRF) outgrew EMX Royalty (EMX), Gold Royalty (GROY), and also Elemental Altus Royalties (OTCQX:ELEMF). And Elemental outgrew Gold Royalty.

April was a great month for the precious metals R&S companies. All the companies, except for Star Royalties (OTCQX:STRFF), recorded a share price growth. The highest one was experienced by Sailfish Royalty (OTCQX:SROYF). Shares of the company jumped by more than 28%. The steep growth started on April 9, but cannot be connected to any company-specific news. On the other hand, Orogen's 21% growth is attributable to the takeover offer by Triple Flag Precious Metals (TFPM). In the case of Star Royalties, the nearly 6% decline is attributable to the disappointing Q4 financial results and the nearly $20 million equity loss from Green Star JV.

Despite a dramatic beginning to the month, gold and also stock markets did very well in the second half of April. The share price of the SPDR Gold Trust ETF (GLD) grew by 5.42%. On the other hand, the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) lost 4.49%. The gold mining stocks did very well, as the VanEck Vectors Gold Miners ETF (GDX) and the VanEck Vectors Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) grew by 6.61% and 7.34% respectively. The precious metals R&S companies did even better. The Precious Metals R&S Index grew by 9.233%, and the Precious Metals R&S Equally Weighted Index grew by 10.83%.

The April News

The majority of April news was related to Q1 operating results. But the biggest news was Triple Flag's $300 million acquisition of Orogen Royalties.

Royal Gold (RGLD) reported Q1 sales of 42,800 toz of gold equivalent (33,300 toz gold, 597,400 toz silver, 1,000 tonnes copper). The company also released its 2024 Asset Handbook.

Osisko Gold Royalties (OR) reported attributable Q1 production of 19,014 toz of gold equivalent. The preliminary revenues amounted to $54.9 million. The company held cash of $63.1 million as of the end of March. The company also released its 2024 sustainability report.

Triple Flag Precious Metals (TFPM) reported Q1 sales of 28,761 toz of gold equivalent (76.3% gold, 23.7% silver). The preliminary revenues reached a new record high of $82.2 million.

On April 22, Triple Flag announced the acquisition of Orogen Royalties (OTCQX:OGNRF) for a total consideration of C$421 million ($304 million), or C$2 ($1.45) per share. The total consideration consists of C$171.5 million in cash, C$171.5 million in shares of Triple Flag, and C$78 million (implied value) in shares of a new company that will hold all assets of Orogen, except for the 1% NSR royalty over the Expanded Silicon project. In other words, Triple Flag arranged the whole deal only to acquire the royalty over AngloGold's (AU) Nevada-located project with inferred and indicated resources of over 16 million toz gold, and growing.

On April 23, Triple Flag announced that it secured amendments to its revolving credit facility. Its size has been expanded from $500 million plus a $200 million accordion to $700 million plus a $300 million accordion. The interest rate equals SOFR + 1.45%-2.75%, and the facility will mature in April 2029.

Sandstorm Gold (SAND) reported Q1 sales of approximately 18,500 toz of gold equivalent, and record-high preliminary revenues of $54.1 million. The company also announced that it purchased and cancelled approximately 3.1 million shares worth around $19 million in Q1.

Gold Royalty (GROY) reported preliminary Q1 sales of 1,249 toz of gold equivalent and revenues of $3.1 million.

EMX Royalty (EMX) announced that it received an early final property payment of $6.85 million from AbraSilver Resource (OTCQX:ABBRF). The payment is related to the Diablillos project, upon which EMX retains a 1% NSR royalty. EMX used the payment and cash on hand, to reduce its long-term debt from $35 million to $25 million.

Vox Royalty (VOXR) announced that Minerals 260, the operator of the Bullabulling gold project, has raised over A$220 million ($140 million), which should help to fund an 80,000 metres drill campaign. The mine should get into production by 2028. Vox holds an A$10/ounce gold royalty over a part of Bullabulling. Vox also provided some news related to its other assets. Probably the most important one is that Black Cat (OTCPK:BLCAF) received approval for underground mining at the Myhree deposit over which Vox holds a 1% NSR royalty. The cash flows from this asset should start coming in 2026.

On April 29, Vox noted that Hecla Mining's Libby Project has been added to the Federal Permitting Improvement Steering Council’s FAST-41 permitting dashboard. This should lead to a shorter and more efficient permitting process. Vox owns a $0.2/ton production royalty over Libby.

Sailfish Royalty (OTCQX:SROYF) released its Q4 financial results. The company recorded revenues of $726,000, and net income of -$602,000.

On April 29, Sailfish announced that it has exercised its option to purchase all remaining silver production from Mako Mining’s (OTCQX:MAKOF) San Albino mine for $1 million. The company also announced that the Final Environmental Impact Statement for the Spring Valley project is expected by July 11, with the Record of Decision expected by August 11. Sailfish holds an up to 3% NSR royalty on Soldius Resources' Spring Valley project with an expected production of around 300,000 toz gold per year, over a more than 10-year long initial mine life.

Orogen Royalties (OTCQX:OGNRF) announced that it entered into a definitive agreement in which Triple Flag will acquire all of the issued and outstanding shares of Orogen for a total consideration of C$421 million ($304 million), or C$2 ($1.45), as mentioned above in more detail.

On April 7, Orogen announced that First Majestic Silver (AG) released an updated reserve and resource estimate for its Ermitano mine. It contains reserves of 4.68 million toz silver, and 199,000 toz gold, as well as resources (including reserves) of nearly 11 million toz silver and 700,000 toz gold. Orogen holds a 2% NSR royalty on Ermitano.

On April 29, Orogen released its Q4 financial results. The company recorded revenues of $3.5 million, and net income of $2.6 million. Compared to the same period of the previous year, the numbers increased by more than 60% and 80% respectively.

Elemental Altus Royalties (OTCQX:ELEMF) reported Q4 revenues of $5.5 million, which is a new record high. The record-high operating cash flow amounted to $3.3 million. However, the net income was only $134,000. In 2025, Elemental expects attributable production of 11,600-13,200 toz of gold equivalent, and revenues of $30.1-34.3 million.

Star Royalties (OTCQX:STRFF) reported Q4 revenues of $100,000, operating cash flow of -$200,000, and net income of -$22.5 million, due to a nearly $20 million equity loss from Green Star JV (Star's share of Green Star's $45.5 million net loss), and $2 million impairment charge related to the Elk royalty. The issues Green Star has to face were presented in the April 28 news release.

Silver Crown Royalties (SCRI:CA) announced the purchase of 1,000 toz of physical silver at a price of $30.65/toz. (Whether this news was worth a separate news release is highly questionable)

On April 28, the company announced that it closed the third tranche of its non-brokered offering of units by issuing 89,400 units for gross proceeds of approximately C$581,100 ($417,000).

The May Outlook

The month of May entered its second half. While the stock market has been recovering nicely, the gold price has retraced from its record highs. This has a negative impact on the share prices of the precious metals R&S companies. For now, it looks like May will end as a negative month for this stock market segment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oxford Lane: Stay Away From This 24% Yield Trap
data.description:
Article Thesis

Oxford Lane Capital Corporation (NASDAQ:OXLC) offers a very hefty 24% dividend yield, but I nevertheless believe that investors shouldn't buy it. OXLC has a bad track record, it doesn't cover its dividend, its net asset value keeps declining, and the valuation, relative to the value of the underlying assets, is too high.

Past Coverage

I have written about Oxford Lane Capital Corporation here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently towards the end of last year, when I published this article. I recommended avoiding OXLC back then, which would have been the correct choice so far -- despite its strong yield, OXLC delivered a negative total return since that article was published, trailing the broad market's return.

Oxford Lane Capital: Recent Earnings Results

Oxford Lane Capital Corporation reported its most recent earnings results earlier this week, with the report being available here. It is important to note that a bad performance during the most recent quarter does not automatically mean that Oxford Lane Capital will perform badly in the current and future quarters as well, but still, looking at how the company did in Q1 gives us a good view about how OXLC is faring in the current environment (interest rates, etc.) and could be a "baseline estimate" for the company's future performance.

In the first quarter, which ended prior to the most recent trade worries around Freedom Day, Oxford Lane Capital saw its net investment income decline substantially compared to the previous quarter as well as compared to the previous year's quarter, at least where it counts -- on a per-share basis. Oxford Lane Capital Corporation issues new shares regularly and invests the proceeds from this share issuance. On a company-wide basis, net investment income was thus up compared to the previous quarter, but I believe that this isn't what matters for investors: When I own 1,000 shares of Oxford Lane Capital, for example, I want to see the net investment income that is attributable to those shares climb -- I do not get any value out of my investment if company-wide net investment is rising but if that is more than offset by share count dilution.

Unfortunately, that is exactly what happened, as core net investment income declined from $0.28 in the previous quarter to $0.23 as of the most recent quarter, a steep fall of around 20%. On a year-over-year basis, the decline was even bigger, as core net investment income had come in at $0.35 one year earlier, meaning the year-over-year decline was roughly 35%. Both the year-over-year trend and the quarter-to-quarter trend point to ongoing headwinds, as profits keep declining on a per-share basis. This negative momentum suggests, I believe, that net investment income could continue to decline on a per-share basis going forward, I thus am not positive regarding OXCL's current and upcoming quarters. But even if Oxford Lane Capital manages to halt the decline and stabilize its net investment income per share at $0.23, it's important to note that this does not cover the company's dividend of $0.09 per month, as this pencils out to a dividend of $0.27 per quarter.

There is no guarantee that the recent trend remains in place, but if net investment income per share were to worsen to the same degree during the current quarter as during the previous quarter, meaning a sequential drop of $0.05, net investment income per share could come in at $0.18 for the current quarter. The dividend payout ratio would climb to 150% in such a scenario. I thus believe that the dividend is far from safe at current prices -- which isn't very surprising, of course, considering the current yield of around 24%, which is a warning sign for income investors.

Oxford Lane Capital has a history of reducing its dividend over time, as we can see in the following chart:

The above chart shows the dividend declaration on a quarterly basis when Oxford Lane Capital made quarterly dividends, which is why the payout of $0.60 does not mean that the dividend was more than six times as high as it is right now. But even when we adjust for that, the dividend a decade ago -- at $0.60 per quarter -- was more than twice as high as the dividend is right now, at $0.27 per quarter. A company that had to reduce its dividend by more than half over the last decade and that has experienced massive share price losses over the same time frame is not anywhere close to being an attractive long-term compounder, I believe.

Looking at Oxford Lane Capital's net asset value performance during the recent past, metrics look similarly bad: Net asset value per share stood at $4.32 as of the end of March, down by a hefty $0.50 from one quarter earlier -- which makes for a decline of more than 10% in just three months. Even worse, net asset value per share continued to drop following the end of the most recent quarter, as management stated that they estimate the company's net asset value per share at around $4.03 as of the end of April. This would mean another decline of 7% in just one month. Net asset value per share may very well stabilize and could even rise in the future if the macro environment would turn very bullish, but the recent trend suggests that OXLC is struggling in the current environment.

Net realized losses totaled $9 million in the most recent quarter, with unrealized depreciation coming in at $19 million for that period. This is before the U.S. is in a recession -- if the U.S. were to enter a recession later this year, e.g., due to trade tensions and tariffs, these losses could climb further. From a risk perspective, I thus do not think that Oxford Lane Capital is well-positioned, or that it is a good investment right here.

Oxford Lane Capital: The Yield Isn't Worth The Risk

Oxford Lane Capital Corporation trades at around $4.50 right now, which makes for a premium of around 10% versus the estimated per-share net asset value as of the end of April. While others may disagree, I do not think that paying a substantial premium to the value of the underlying assets makes sense here, considering this value has been shrinking quickly in recent months and considering that these assets could be quite risky during a potential recession later this year. If Oxford Lane was trading at a substantial discount to its net asset value, I could see it as a contrarian investment -- but paying way more than the market value of these assets does not seem like a great deal to me.

A high dividend yield can be enticing, and Oxford Lane Capital currently offers a high dividend yield for sure. But this dividend isn't covered for now, which could result in a dividend cut in the future -- which would not be the first such cut.

In combination with declining per-share asset values, risks during a potential economic downturn, and factoring in OXLC's meager total return track record, I do not consider this high-yield investment attractive right here. One could even call it a yield trap, I believe, considering the risk of a dividend cut and the substantial value erosion both on a NAV basis and on a share price basis. I wouldn't short it, but consider it a "stay away/better watch it from the sidelines" type of investment.

data.title: Intel Doesn't Need To Beat TSMC To Win - Nearing $20 Buy Signal
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) is our swing trade safe haven in what is a messy market, plagued by uncertainty and desperation for relief. Our strategy is to buy Intel under $20 and sell above the $23 level and rinse and repeat until Q4 approaches when we’ll hold on to that below $20 and ride the 18A tailwind into 2026 with the node scheduled for production in Q4 and mass production in 2026.

These chips have backside power and Gate-All-Around and are the first of their kind in the industry (yes, not even Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) is working with backside power, meaning by the time INTC is working on its second-gen of 18A, TSMC will be on its first rendition). Listen, we’re not throwing shade at TSMC; we’re expecting TSMC to catch up in no time and continue to dominate the advanced node market, but we believe INTC is building itself up to be a competitor on two fronts: PC Client and Foundry.

Intel doesn’t need to beat TSMC to win

The misconception about Intel is that it needs to outperform TSMC’s 2N in order to gain share or be a competitive foundry, but that’s not what will happen. If Intel can successfully produce 18A, then it’s already on the map because of two things.

  1. The first has to do with Intel’s internal business, which will be where the node is actually used. Panther Lake running on 18A means Intel gets a competitive edge against Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), which it has been struggling to grab for a while now. So, benefit number one is making its own CPUs more competitive and being able to leverage upside in PC Client, which is +55% of its total sales. The reality is that Intel is getting screwed by AMD; CPU-tracking firm Mercury Research report shows that Intel is growing its share of the market extremely slowly when compared to AMD with a sequential growth of 0.3% in Q1 to 75.6% compared to AMD growing 3.6% to a share of 24.4%. The takeaway, according to McCarron, is that “AMD’s growth rate in the quarter was multiples of Intel’s.” And, Intel isn’t hiding it. Intel is getting more aggressive with pricing to compete until 18A is here. The company recently slashed prices on its Arrow Lake CPU family, which it launched in 2H24. Intel Core Ultra 7 265K and Core Ultra 7 265KF have seen a price drop of 26% versus their MSRP, according to WCCFTech, and will now be priced at an additional discount of $100. The prices of Core Ultra 7 265K and Core Ultra 7 265KF are now $299 and $284, compared to earlier pricing of $399 and $384, respectively. This is a sign that yes, Intel is getting share pressure from AMD and they know it. Our belief is that 18A could enable Intel to gain share again. Remember, Intel once held over 85% of the market. So, it’s a timing game.
  2. The second benefit is that it’ll put Intel on the map as a reliable foundry player; customers don’t approach Intel now because it’s too risky, and in the current macro backdrop, it's an unaffordable risk. Even if Intel doesn’t gain foundry customers on the back of this, the chatter of that potential alone will carry the stock. The market isn’t actually expecting Intel to attract customers for its foundry business, and Intel is to thank for that. The company actually came out and said they have limited customer commitments. CFO David Zinser noted that the company is seeing limited adoption for its high-end nodes. This means that the high-end nodes would be predominantly used for internal products. The positive of having external customers is that it would allow Intel to scale its high-end nodes and increase its fab utilization by thereby improving margins. Low expectations make for more dramatic stock price reactions when better-than-feared news breaks, and we think that could be the case with 18A’s successful ramp in Q4 and early 1H26. TSMC’s production lines are now extremely crowded, even from Intel, which is outsourcing its 3nm ramp now, and that should make an easier window for customers to look at Intel’s 18A once the company proves it works by using it in internal products.

Who doesn’t love the underdog?

Arguably, the best part about Intel is that it's cheap because we’re looking at it early: valuation-wise, Intel stock trades at a Price-to-Sales of 1.76x, 42% lower than the peer group average, and Price-to-Book of 0.95x, also lower than the group average of 3.37x. Not to mention, the market is betting against Intel, which means expectations have been dragged down substantially. Over the past three months, Intel has seen 32 downward revisions in EPS compared to one upward revision. Intel is the classic underdog story in the current semi market; its share on the PC Client front is getting eaten away by AMD, it can’t compete with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) on the AI server front, and it’s looking up to TSMC to better run its foundry business. It’s delusional to expect Intel to suddenly dominate or even compete meaningfully in these three markets, but we bet that two will be enough: PC Client and Foundry. The two of these fit together with the same catalyst to charge Intel’s comeback: 18A.

We liked Tan at Computex

This Computex was the first for Lip-Bu Tan as CEO of Intel; he wasn’t loud about it. Tan prioritized an intimate dinner with the company’s supply chain in China over a spot line appearance at the conference itself. He also signaled that he is more than well-positioned with Taiwan supply chain partners; he noted in his keynote:

Taiwan is not just a special place for me personally… It is the undisputed global hub for semiconductor innovation and a cornerstone of the world's technology supply chain.

Tan's whole motto is that he’s committed to “under-promise and over-deliver,” and that’s exactly what you need to push the stock price higher.

This week, the impact of Tan at Computex isn’t showing up on the stock, but that’s a broader trend of the relief rally of the U.S. and China striking a deal calming down. Over the past five days, Intel is down almost 3%, and we’re edging closer to the $20 level, which is where we think investors should start looking at the stock.

What to do with the stock?

Intel could be back at the $40s level after 18A is successfully out; it’ll expand margins, enable share regain on the PC Client front, and put the company on the map as an advanced foundry player. Intel won’t beat TSMC, but it doesn’t need to; it can just show up, and the chatter of a successful 18A ramp should be enough to carry the stock. Intel is a buy in our books under $20 per share.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amazon: Tariffs Will Strengthen The E-Commerce Empire
data.description:
Some investors might be wondering if it is too late to buy the stock of Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) given that it has already recovered much of its losses along with the broader market. The company continues to benefit both from generative AI tailwinds to its cloud computing business, while also showing that it can increase the dominance of its e-commerce business through faster shipping speeds without sacrificing on profitability. The company is solidly profitable and maintains a net cash balance sheet.

While the near term might see some volatility to the e-commerce business due to the tariff escalations, I do not see anything stopping this growth engine over the long term. If anything, this looks like an opportunity for the company to lean into its financial and competitive strengths and take market share. I reiterate my Strong Buy rating for the stock.

AMZN Stock Price

I last covered AMZN in March where I stuck with my bullishness amidst a bursting AI bubble. The stock has returned 8% since then.

While some may be of the opinion that stocks have not fallen enough to account for the new economic risks, AMZN looks quite attractive here.

AMZN Stock Key Metrics

The e-commerce and cloud titan delivered strong Q1 results even amidst tough macro conditions. Revenues grew 9% YoY to $155.7 billion, coming in ahead of the high end of guidance of between $151 billion and $155.5 billion.

Operating income came in at $18.4 billion, also exceeding the high end of guidance of between $14 billion and $18 billion. Tariffs do not appear to have impacted either top line growth nor profitability, but it remains to be seen if there will be an impact in the remainder of the year.

The company saw its North America e-commerce segment deliver stunning 8% YoY growth with operating margins expanding 40 bps to 6.2%. The trailing twelve months operating margin stood at 6.5%, which is incredible given that the company is operating at peak shipping speeds. It is now clear that expedited shipping has not impeded the company’s ability to drive profits, allowing it to fully benefit from the associated competitive advantages.

AMZN continued to deliver solid profitable growth in its international operations, but is now lapping tougher comparables.

The cloud computing segment AWS generated 17% YoY growth, an incredible growth rate given the $120 billion annual run rate. The company also showed off the profit potential of this segment, with operating margins expanding to 39%.

AMZN ended the quarter with $95 billion of cash versus $53.4 billion of debt, representing a bulletproof net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management guided for the second quarter to see up to 11% YoY revenue growth. While the tariff uncertainty may make it difficult to outperform on the top-line, I find it likely that the company outperforms on the profit guidance given that it posted an 11.8% operating margin this past quarter.

On the conference call, management noted that they “set new delivery speed records” as they delivered “more items in the same day or next day” than any other quarter in their history. Management noted exposure to the ongoing tariff negotiations, noting that many of their retailers are either buying directly from China or “buying from companies who themselves are buying from China.” Management noted that their tariff impact is “not large” as of the next quarter due to them having done “a lot of pre-buying” in the first quarter, but I expect costs to rise more significantly in the third quarter.

I would not be surprised if AMZN tries to eat some of the costs in order to continue taking market share, which would likely pressure operating margins in the near term. While the de minimis tariff was reduced to 54% down from 120%, this still reflects a significantly larger number than the prior exemption and may affect the company for several quarters until it begins lapping these comparables.

Is AMZN Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

AMZN was recently in the news due to Nike (NKE) deciding to return to the platform for the first time since 2019. While NKE is just one retailer and thus is unlikely to make a significant impact on its own, it does help contribute to the narrative that AMZN has become synonymous with retail. I expect other name brands to follow suit as they could tap into the large market funnel and fast shipping speeds.

As of recent prices, AMZN traded hands at just around 32x earnings.

That looks like a reasonable valuation given that consensus estimates call for high-single-digit top-line growth over the coming years.

At this point, it is clear that both the e-commerce and cloud businesses are high quality with the company holding strong competitive footing (curiously enough both of these had been out of favor at some point by investors over the past 5 years). I value AWS at a 12x sales multiple, placing the valuation at $1.6 trillion. I value the e-commerce business at a 2.7x sales multiple (equating to a 30x earnings multiple based on 9% long term margins), placing the valuation at around $1.6 trillion.

Summing these up, this leads to an AMZN valuation of around $290 per share. I can see upside to both of these estimates, especially as both make further progress on margin expansion.

AMZN Stock Risks

While I expect AWS to perform strongly even in a recession, the e-commerce operations may be particularly exposed to ongoing tariff impacts. It remains unclear how much the online marketplace might be disrupted by elevated import costs from China and others. While the numbers thus far have not shown impact, as a consumer myself my hunch says “a lot” of the products are likely sourced from China. It is plausible, if not likely, that the company ends up seeing material near term impact as its sellers might need to re-think their sourcing logistics, and buyers adapt to potentially higher sticker prices.

That said, I expect the company’s dominant market positioning to enable it to return to growth mode over the long term. I note that the AWS segment continues to face intense competition from other cloud titans, and it isn’t clear if generative AI ends up leading to a “winner takes most” scenario.

AMZN Stock Conclusion

The company is showing resilient top-line growth while doing right by investors in prioritizing profit margins. Both revenue and profit growth might be pressured in the near term as the e-commerce business grapples with new tariff costs, but the company’s dominant competitive positioning puts it in a position of strength from which it might even be able to accelerate its market share gains during this difficult period.

The stock finally looks cheap on conventional value metrics due to the significant gains on profitability - I see it as just a matter of time before the company ends up ramping up its share repurchase program, which might help bring more investor attention to the name. It's worth noting that the stock even was recently added by famed value investor Bill Ackman. The stock still looks cheap even based on reasonable assumptions in my sum-of-the-parts analysis, suggesting further upside.

AMZN is a core position at the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my Strong Buy rating for AMZN stock.

data.title: SCHQ: Avoiding The Twin Dollar-Duration Risk
data.description:
The Schwab Long-Term U.S. Treasury ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHQ) is a rather long duration ETF. At these effective maturities is precisely where the considerable increases in interest rates are occurring. While a de-inversion of the yield curve may ordinarily be seen as a positive thing, we believe that it is coming down to several deleterious changes to the economic monetary order, and that the increase in long-term rates is a consequence of compounding worries for the US budget interacting with a monetarily dangerous political isolationism in the US that threatens utility of the dollar. The weak auction signals some of these worries, as well as the disconnect in the rally of US stocks versus the stagnating USD FX rate. Intermediate duration might be alright given the muted expectations we discussed in other coverage, but we're being careful about higher duration exposures.

SCHQ Breakdown

The effective duration of the SCHQ is 14.2 years, which is on the long side.

The yield curve has appreciably translated upwards on the longer maturities over the last 12 months, and especially in the last 6 months. The movements have been significant and changes in this part of the yield curve mean that by definition there has been a shift in how the bond market is evaluating structural factors. Inflation has been relatively muted in the latest readings, but this concerns mostly the 1-3 year outlook where there has been an overall downward translation as opposed to upwards, however for the structural factors:

  • Bond markets are likely looking at tariffs and considering the structural inflationary effect that comes from deglobalisation and trade disintegration, even if it's not total and just incremental compared to prior expectations that likely assumed globalisation with no caveats.
  • Also related to the tariffs and much more importantly is the threat to the dollar that has been caused by not only the tariffs themselves but their erratic communication. There is a general reticence towards the US at this point as trading partners have been feeling punished, particularly countries that are otherwise allies. This goes for allocators who buy dollars as well, as the lack of recovery of the dollar versus a recovery in marginal demand for US stocks to pre-Liberation Day levels reflects a reticence to come back to dollars even though major tariffs have been paused for now and many believe that quite a bit of the tariffs will be walked back. Economically, the logic would be that a tariff would make importing less appealing and therefore spare the dollar from additional sales to import products, thereby strengthening the dollar. The fact that did not happen imputes that a decline in the geopolitical power of the dollar offset the benefits of protectionism to lead to the USD's overall decline versus peer currencies like the EUR. Countries don't buy and use dollars because they believe in the US budget and the solvency of the US. It is critical that the USD maintain its utility as a reserve currency for the state of the viability of the US budget and balance sheet to be ignored.
  • The downgrade of US debt by Moody's (MCO) highlights the runaway debt (exacerbated also by new spending bills), and means that confidence and common use of the dollar is more important than ever to maintain its utility and prevent it from being evaluated like any other currency, which usually depends on the health of the sovereign balance sheet. The weak bond auction was yet another worrying indicator that dollar has taken a reputation hit (decades to build trust, a day to lose it) and that the deficit is not being properly addressed and is becoming a factor. In the worst case scenario this may accelerate transitions by some countries in adopting another effective standard currency, where the emergence of another reasonably solid currency, particularly among US allies, could be a danger to the current monetary world order.
  • Related to the above fact, the longer-term maturities are more concerned with the ability of the US to continue to refinance its debt at attractive rates, and for the deficit to not get out of control, or for at least the conditions where an out of control deficit can be ignored by USD investors (thereby assuring indefinite refinancing more driven by the demand for the utility that the USD offers as opposed to the soundness of a USD investment). We believe the markets are considering threats to the reserve currency status to some extent, even if it's just a remote risk, in the considerable re-evaluation of long-term yields.

Bottom Line

We are not trying to make a call on the USD reserve currency status. We just want to highlight that the US's finances depends on the utility of the USD as a reserve currency, which requires that the USD remains broadly in demand, in particular by foreign buyers who both buy USD to sell their currencies for the US to net import and maintain their current account deficit and financial account surplus. The discrepancy between stock and currency recovery post Liberation Day is a worry in that regard as it reflects the damage that might have already been done as a consequence of communication style, cemented by the actual fact that overall tariff rates in the US now are akin to Smoot-Hawley levels, with the US being much more import-led now than they were back then. The pause on the China tariffs has been positive news and the market was correct to receive the news as such - after all the US and Chinese current and financial accounts integrated together look very balanced and healthy. The USD is still, of course, the reserve currency standard for now and in use, but the general threat to major trade relationships that have been an assumption of decades of intense capital investment means significant impairment risk to the global business environment. For long duration bets like SCHQ, investors would be taking a risk on what could become a more common talking point. The whole market, bonds and stocks, in all developed markets, already depend quite a lot on the current monetary order which depends on the USD reserve currency status. We would not want to take an additional risk by betting directly on sensitive, long duration bonds that have a more encompassing exposure to the USD reserve currency status. There are some select markets where you can get out of the way of these risks and we'd focus on those.
